title: Te Whakahaumanu o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Restoring the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds deep cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and local kaumātua speak of its historical role as a mahinga kai and spiritual pathway. Students will connect with the pūrākau of the stream while applying scientific methods to assess its current state.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific equipment and methods
Explain how human activities in our local catchment area affect stream health using evidence from investigations
Compare traditional Māori knowledge of stream management with modern scientific approaches to freshwater restoration
Propose evidence-based solutions for improving the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku that incorporate both mātauranga Māori and science

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia at the streamside, followed by a welcome from a local kaumātua who will share the pūrākau and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students create observational sketches and notes about the stream environment, recording initial questions and observations.

activities.title: He Tātari Wai - Water Quality Testing
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and analyze water samples from three points along the stream (upstream, mid-stream, near confluence). They measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and dissolved oxygen, recording data in field journals and comparing results to healthy stream benchmarks.

activities.title: Te Raupapa Rauropi - Macroinvertebrate Survey
activities.description: Students conduct a stream bug hunt using kick nets and identification charts to assess biodiversity. They learn how different species indicate water quality (e.g., presence of mayflies vs. snails) and calculate a stream health score using the Macroinvertebrate Community Index method.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero o Neherā - Traditional Knowledge Integration
activities.description: Through guided discussion with kaumātua and resource materials, students explore traditional Māori practices for protecting waterways (rahui, tapu, monitoring through natural signs). They create a Venn diagram comparing these approaches with their scientific findings.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Anamata - Future Solutions Hui
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop restoration proposals for the stream that combine scientific evidence with cultural principles. They present their proposals to the class in a mock community hui format, justifying their recommendations with data and cultural reasoning.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council Water Quality Testing Kits and Macroinvertebrate Identification Guides
Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' resources and data sheets
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Māori Freshwater Fishing' and local iwi environmental management plans

tasks: Complete a detailed stream health investigation report with data tables and graphs
Create a comparative analysis of scientific and mātauranga Māori approaches to stream monitoring
Develop and present a culturally-informed restoration plan for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Understanding about science
Living World: Ecology - Investigate the interdependence of living things in an ecosystem
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its implications for life
Science in the New Zealand Curriculum: Developing and understanding of the role of science in relation to the environment and society
